entries.title: About the Course
entries.content: Get to know the diverse people who inhabited North America before Columbus arrived. Learn how they used what was available around them for food, shelter, clothing, and protection. Observe their customs and hear their stories.

rows.label: History: Grade 1 (U.S.)
rows.books: Frozen Land||Hiawatha and the Peacemaker||Itse Selu: Cherokee Harvest Festival||Moonstick: The Seasons of the Sioux||Tales the Totems Tell||The Book of Indians||The Very First Americans

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

History: Grade 1 (U.S.)
☐ NOTE: Hiawatha & the Peacemaker contains some illustrations that sensitive children may find scary; however, this book is very important, as it tells the traditional legend of how the Iroquois Confederacy (which heavily influenced the American Founding Fathers) was formed. Preview the book and decide if there are pictures you would rather your student(s) not see.

☐ Go through the lessons and decide if you want to print the images provided in the links. Store these with lesson plans for easy access.

☐ Number the pages (starting with the first page of text) in The Very First Americans, Frozen Land, Moonstick, Hiawatha & The Peacemaker, and Itse Selu.

☐ Print the PDFs linked below in Quick Links for Tales the Totems Tell and The Book of Indians, Chapter IX.

books.title: Moonstick: The Seasons of the Sioux

blocks.text:
• Read through the lesson and gather any materials or find any necessary links. 

• If you have not already read the book, you may want to read the selected pages in advance.

• Connect to previous lessons by asking students to recall where they left off. 

• If students have difficulty recalling, teachers might try showing them a picture from the reading, sharing something the teacher remembers, or prompting them with the first sentence of the last reading. This will often refresh their memory enough to allow them to recall.

• If students still cannot recall anything from the last reading, teachers might consider continuing anyway (with a shorter reading this time) or repeating the previous lesson, depending on the reading and the nature of the book.

• Use a picture, map, or engaging question to pique the student's interest in the day's lesson. 

• If necessary, you may talk about one or two unfamiliar words that will be crucial to understanding.

• Read the day's passage out loud. When reading to pre-readers, point to the words as you read, even if it seems like they aren't paying attention.

• Move at the student’s pace for the lesson time rather than a particular number of pages. It is vitally important that teachers not try to ‘force feed’ a student more than they can consume in one sitting.

• Students who have a difficult time sitting still or listening may benefit from a fidget of their choice, such as a piece of putty or clay or an afghan; flexible positioning, such as a wobble seat or inverted sitting; or sensory modulation, such as a weighted vest or blanket or adjustment of light, temperature, or sound.

• Have students retell the passage orally.

• Alternatively, learners may use words, pictures, Legos, dolls, etc., to process and convey ideas in the manner most natural to them.  

• Teachers may take turns to model.

• Narration is a complex skill requiring the coordination of several parts of the brain. If a student struggles to narrate in general, with a specific subject or book, or on one day but not others, then this is an opportunity to learn. Observe when they struggle with this or any other activity today. Notice facial expressions and body language; listen to what they say about why it is hard or how they feel about it. Take note of these observations for later, tell them that you see how hard they are working, and then offer whatever support they need to end on a positive note. If necessary, take a break for the day.

• Give the student an opportunity to share thoughts on the lesson and any connections made.  

• To stimulate discussion, you could ask them what they thought about the passage or ask an open-ended question.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
The First Americans
⍞ Materials: The Very First Americans, Globe

➜ INTRO
Point to Iraq (Tigris and Euphrates Rivers) on a globe. This is where the Garden of Eden is believed to have been. From there, where could people spread out and walk as the population grew? Point to North America. Could they walk here? How do you think they might have gotten here?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.1 "Long, long ago" - "The name stuck."

➜ LOCATE
Find the Bering Strait on the globe.
lessons.teacherNotes: • ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
People of the Tundra
⍞ Materials: Frozen Land, Globe

➜ RECAP
What do you remember from what we read last time?

➜ INTRO
When the first people came to America, some stayed in the frozen north (see picture). Humans need food, water, shelter, and clothing to survive. How might you get these things if you lived here? Find the Hudson Bay on a globe. This is where our story takes place. Is it near where you live?
∞ Image Link: Frozen Tundra

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.1-8 "As the sun" - "be a fish."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
Caribou and Other Animals
⍞ Materials: Frozen Land

➜ INTRO
What kinds of animals do you think live near Kenalogak? What do you think they might eat?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.9-16 "Kenalogak loves" - "the wolf skin."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

• NATURE NOTEBOOK - Prompt in Outdoor Work Quick Link: Go to a natural area near your home. Imagine you are a Native American living in that place. Notice what is around you. How would you find or make food, water, shelter, and clothing so you could survive?

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
Making an Igloo
⍞ Materials: Frozen Land

➜ RECAP
What do you remember about the Inuit?

➜ INTRO
What do you like to do when you spend time with your grandparents? Are there any special stories or songs they like to share? Today, Kenalogak's grandparents are coming for a visit.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.17-27 "Playing outside" - "snow and ice."

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
The Owl and the Lemming

➜ INTRO
This is a "trickster tale" from the Eskimo/Inuit people. Lots of cultures have these kinds of stories. In the video, you will hear Inuit language and music. See if you can figure out why this kind of story is called a "trickster tale."

➜ VIEW & NARRATE
∞ Video Link: The Owl and the Lemming
lessons.teacherNotes:
• ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
People of the Pacific Northwest
⍞ Materials: The Very First Americans

➜ INTRO
Not all of the first Americans stayed in the frozen north. Look at this picture. How is the land different from where Kenalogak lives? What do you think people who settled here may have used for food, water, shelter, and clothing?
∞ Image Link: Pacific Northwest

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.3-8 "Tribes like the Chinook," - "rich he was."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Remember to recap the previous lesson before starting the new lesson.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Comparing Two Ways of Life
⍞ Materials: The Very First Americans

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Study the picture. Talk about some of the differences you notice between how these people live and how Kenalogak's family lives. If Kenalogak came for a visit to this village, what might she find strange? If one of these children were to visit Kenalogak, what would she need to do differently to survive? 
p.5-6
lessons.teacherNotes: • ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
Totem Poles
⍞ Materials: Globe

➜ INTRO
Find British Columbia on a globe. Is it very close to Hudson Bay? Is it close to where you live? The people we will learn about today live here.

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
In this video, we learn about totem poles and other parts of the culture of the Pacific Northwest Tribes.
∞ Video Link: Pacific Northwest People
lessons.teacherNotes: • ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons. Try illustrating a totem pole.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 9
lessons.body:
Totem Poles
⍞ Materials: Tales the Totems Tell (PDF)  
∞ PDF Link: Tales the Totems Tell

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"The First Totem Pole" p.1-9
"When Ramlaryaelk" - "the totem pole."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Remember to recap the previous lesson before starting the new lesson.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
Totem Poles (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Tales the Totems Tell (PDF)  
∞ PDF Link: Tales the Totems Tell

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"The Raven and the Indians" p.10-17
"Of all the" - "touched the moon."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 11
lessons.body:
Catch-Up Day

➜ CATCH UP
Use this time to catch up on lessons in this or another course, or explore Extra Helpings.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 12
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course. If a book was not finished during the term, adjust the exam questions to reflect what was read or allow students to tell about or draw from stories or excerpts that they remember. If a student has difficulty getting started or gets "stuck," they may look back at illustrations to help them refocus on ideas.

Questions will come from:
The Very First Americans, Frozen Land, Tales the Totems Tell

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
People of the Southwest
⍞ Materials: The Very First Americans 

➜ INTRO
How is the place in this picture different from the Pacific Northwest? If your Tribe lived here, how could you get food, water, shelter, and clothing? The tall rock formation that is flat on top is called a "mesa," which means "table" in Spanish.
∞ Image Link: Desert Mesa

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.9-12 "The hot, dry" - "with the rain."
lessons.teacherNotes: • ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons. Try illustrating a mesa.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 14
lessons.body:
Comparing Two Ways of Life
⍞ Materials: The Very First Americans 

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Study the picture and tell what you notice. How are these people similar to the ones in the Pacific Northwest? How are they different? Count how many clay pots you see. Why do you think these people made pots of clay instead of boxes of wood for holding things? Find something that has been decorated with a design. How are the designs different from the designs used in the Pacific Northwest?
p.9-10, 5-8

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 15
lessons.body:
People of the Mesas
⍞ Materials: The Book of Indians (PDF)
∞ PDF Link: The Book of Indians 

➜ INTRO
This is a story about a little boy who lives in the Southwest. One thing we have not read about yet is what these people ate. Listen for types of food.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"Little-Turtle of the Cliff Dwellers" Ch.IX p.81-83
"Little-Turtle" - "time will tell."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Remember to recap the previous lesson before starting the new lesson.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 16
lessons.body:
People of the Mesas (cont.)
⍞ Materials: The Book of Indians (PDF)
∞ PDF Link: The Book of Indians 

➜ INTRO
What foods do you remember Little-Turtle's people eating? Where do you think the people of the mesa got their water? Listen today for several sources.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"Little-Turtle of the Cliff Dwellers" Ch.IX p.83-85
"When Uncle Antelope" - "out of the canyon."

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 17
lessons.body:
People of the Mesas (cont.) 
⍞ Materials: The Book of Indians (PDF)
∞ PDF Link: The Book of Indians 

➜ INTRO
Do you remember what was happening when we left off?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
"Little-Turtle of the Cliff Dwellers" Ch.IX p.85-88
"How odd," - "saved his people."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 18
lessons.body:
People of the Plains & Prairies
⍞ Materials: The Very First Americans, Globe

➜ INTRO
The Great Plains is a huge region that cuts right through the middle of the United States. Find it on a map or globe. Do you live here? Look at the picture and think about the Native Americans who lived there. What might they use for food, water, shelter, and clothing?
∞ Image Link: The Great Plains

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.13-18 "Long ago," - "rolled-up hair."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Remember to recap the previous lesson before starting the new lesson.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 19
lessons.body:
Comparing Ways of Life
⍞ Materials: The Very First Americans

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS 
Look at the pictures. Tell all you notice. Why did tipis work well for these people? Would they have worked as well on the mesa, in the Northwest, or in the tundra? What designs and decorations do you notice? Look at the women cooking. One woman is heating a stone in the fire. When it is hot, she will drop it in the "pot" to cook the food. What do you suppose the pot is made of?
p.13-18

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 20
lessons.body:
Keeping Time: Moons
⍞ Materials: Moonstick: The Seasons of the Sioux

➜ INTRO
How many months are in a year? Can you say them all? Native Americans had their own way of marking time using what they observed in nature. One "moon" was the time from one full moon to the next. How many moons do you think there are in a year? Read the last page to find out.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.1-8 "Moon of the Birth of Calves" - "Moon of the Ripe Juneberries"

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 21
lessons.body:
Keeping Time: Moons (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Moonstick: The Seasons of the Sioux

➜ INTRO
Do you remember how Native Americans named each moon? If you were going to name the moon you are in now, according to what is happening in nature, what name might you choose?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.9-16 "Cherry-Ripening Moon" - "Moon of the Falling Leaves"
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Remember to recap the previous lesson before starting the new lesson.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 22
lessons.body:
Keeping Time: Moons (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Moonstick: The Seasons of the Sioux

➜ INTRO
Look back at the pictures so far. Which seasons have you seen?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.17-24 "Moon of the Hairless Calves" - "Sore-Eyes Moon"
lessons.teacherNotes:
• ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 23
lessons.body:
Keeping Time: Moons (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Moonstick: The Seasons of the Sioux

➜ INTRO
Native Americans still live in the USA. What are some ways you think their lives may have changed?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.25-30 "Moon When the Grass Comes Up" - "it is so arranged."

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 24
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course. If a book was not finished during the term, adjust the exam questions to reflect what was read or allow students to tell about or draw from stories or excerpts that they remember. If a student has difficulty getting started or gets "stuck," they may look back at illustrations to help them refocus on ideas.

Questions will come from:
The Very First Americans, The Book of Indians (Ch.IX), Moonstick

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
People of the Woodlands
⍞ Materials: The Very First Americans

➜ INTRO
East of the Great Plains, the USA was once covered in forest. How do you think life in the forest might have been different from life in the desert or on the plains?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.19-22 "The Delaware," - "on their prey."

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Look at the picture. How do these people appear similar to and different from others you have read about?

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
The Iroquois Confederacy
⍞ Materials: Hiawatha and the Peacemaker

PREP: Read "Historical Note" on p.43.

➜ INTRO
Point to the Great Lakes on a map or globe. This story takes place on the eastern shores, where there were five Tribes who were constantly at war with one another.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
p.1-10 "A fierce scream" - "great Peacemaker."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Remember to recap the previous lesson before starting the new lesson.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 27
lessons.body:
The Iroquois Confederacy (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Hiawatha and the Peacemaker

➜ INTRO
Can you remember what was happening when we left off?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.11-18 "I stared at" - "as two nations."

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 28
lessons.body:
The Iroquois Confederacy (cont.) 
⍞ Materials: Hiawatha and the Peacemaker

➜ INTRO: What is "forgiveness?" Have you ever forgiven or been forgiven by someone? How did it make you feel?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.19-28 "Guided by the moon" - "as four nations."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 29
lessons.body:
The Iroquois Confederacy (cont.) 
⍞ Materials: Hiawatha and the Peacemaker

➜ INTRO
What do you think is going to happen when they meet Tadodaho?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.29-36 "The wicked" - "where they belonged."

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
The Iroquois Confederacy (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Hiawatha and the Peacemaker

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.37-42 "Everyone followed" - "tree of peace."

➜ DISCUSS
Share a little from the Historical Note on p.43, particularly that the United States' Founding Fathers were influenced by the Iroquois Confederacy when they were setting up our government.
lessons.teacherNotes: • ILLUSTRATE - Draw a scene from the story using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 31
lessons.body:
The Cherokee
⍞ Materials: Itse Selu

PREP: Read the Preface.

➜ INTRO
Far south of the Great Lakes, where it is not so cold, the Cherokee lived. Find the Blue Ridge Mountains on a map or globe. This was their home before people came from Europe. Do you live near here?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.1-8 "Little Wolf blinked" - "her work done."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Remember to recap the previous lesson before starting the new lesson.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 32
lessons.body:
The Cherokee (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Itse Selu

➜ INTRO
Look at the pictures on p.2 of Itse Selu and p.18 of The Very First Americans. How are the cooking methods similar? How are they different?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.9-18 "Little Wolf gobbled" - "stop rolling."

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 33
lessons.body:
The Cherokee (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Itse Selu

➜ INTRO
Does your family have a special tradition to celebrate the New Year? Today's reading is about a very special Cherokee New Year's tradition, including a trickster tale. Do you remember what that means?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.19-28 "The late afternoon" - "Happy New Year."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• ILLUSTRATE - Draw the story of the clever rabbit and the foolish wildcat (p.23) using watercolor or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

★ TEACHER TIP
Students should also be encouraged to narrate using Legos, puppets, or by acting out the scene.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 34
lessons.body:
Comparing Cultures

➜ DISCUSS
Both the Iroquois and the Cherokee Tribes lived in the forest. How were their appearances similar and different? What about their houses and clothing? Look back through the pictures of your books and talk about how each group of people used the materials that were available to them for food, water, shelter, and clothing. Which group lived nearest you?

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 35
lessons.body:
The Coming of the Europeans
⍞ Materials: The Very First Americans

➜ INTRO
The native people lived as the stories/books we've read described for many years. Then something happened that would change their way of life forever. What do you think it was?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.23-29 "Most woodlands" - "very first Americans."
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Remember to recap the previous lesson before starting the new lesson.

lessons.title: 10m History: Grade 1 (U.S.) - Lesson 36
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course. If a book was not finished during the term, adjust the exam questions to reflect what was read or allow students to tell about or draw from stories or excerpts that they remember. If a student has difficulty getting started or gets "stuck," they may look back at illustrations to help them refocus on ideas.

Questions will come from:
The Very First Americans, Hiawatha and the Peacemaker, Itse Selu
